description: insert_me mutation strategy catalog. Each entry describes one CWE family, its current implementation maturity, and the mutation strategies available or planned.

strategies.description: Transforms malloc(<expr>) to malloc((<expr>) - 1). Introduces a one-byte undercount in a heap allocation. When the caller writes the expected number of bytes, the write overflows the allocated region.
strategies.notes: Most reliable strategy. Always produces a syntactically valid, single-flaw mutation. Scores 0.75 in quality gate (arithmetic-expression malloc lines).

strategies.description: Inserts free(<ptr>); immediately before a pointer dereference (ptr->field or *ptr). The freed pointer is then used on the very next line, producing a use-after-free.
strategies.notes: Reliable strategy with multi-signal scoring (prior malloc in scope, loop-body and conditional-guard penalties, sub-malloc penalty). Scores 0.65-0.90 depending on context.

strategies.description: Inserts a duplicate free(<ptr>); immediately before an existing free() call. The heap allocator receives two calls to free the same pointer, which is undefined behaviour and exploitable on most platforms.
strategies.notes: Works on any simple free(ptr) or free(ptr->field) call. Quality gate scores ACCEPT_WITH_NOTES (score 0.55 — lower than CWE-122/416 because free_call scoring is simpler). Loop-body and guard penalties applied in seeder.

strategies.cwe_name: Missing Release of Memory after Effective Lifetime
strategies.description: Replaces a free(<ptr>); call with a comment /* CWE-401: free(ptr) removed - memory leak */. The heap-allocated object is never released, causing a memory leak.
strategies.notes: Simple and reliable. Quality gate scores ACCEPT_WITH_NOTES. Detectors often flag this as low-severity; semantic matching may be needed for accurate evaluation.

strategies.description: Remove the null-check guard before a pointer dereference. Target pattern: 'if (!ptr) return;' followed by 'ptr->field'. Mutation: remove or invert the null check.
strategies.notes: Requires multi-line awareness (guard + deref). The Patcher currently operates on single lines; would need a two-line mutation mode.

strategies.description: Replace a size guard expression with one that overflows. Target: malloc(n * sizeof(T)) where n is an integer parameter. Mutation: remove the overflow check (e.g., if (n > MAX) return;) that precedes the allocation.
strategies.notes: Requires detecting the overflow guard pattern before a multiplication-based malloc. Multi-line mutation needed. Juliet has rich coverage of this CWE.

strategies.description: Similar to alloc_size_undercount but targets fixed-size stack or heap buffers written via memcpy/strcpy. Mutation: increase the write count beyond the allocation.
strategies.notes: Overlaps with CWE-122 for heap targets. Stack targets would require detecting local array declarations, which is harder without an AST.

strategies.description: Transform an allocation to be smaller than the read size. Read operations (memcpy src, array index) that follow would access beyond the allocation.
strategies.notes: Single-line patcher sufficient if the read call and size expression are on one line. Detection difficulty varies: some tools miss reads vs. writes.

strategies.description: Replace a safe printf(fmt, args) call with printf(user_input) where user_input is the variable from the first argument, removing the format string literal.
strategies.notes: string_operation pattern_type exists. The mutation is simple string replacement but requires distinguishing format-string printf from direct-string printf. Feasible without AST.

strategies.description: Replace strcpy(dst, src) with a write that exceeds the stack buffer size. Requires detecting the stack buffer declaration to know its size.
strategies.notes: CANDIDATE but non-trivial: buffer size detection requires backward scan for declaration. Without AST, fragile. Deferred.

strategies.description: Remove a zero-divisor guard (e.g., if (denom == 0) return;) before an integer division.
strategies.notes: Pattern detection is simple (integer division with a preceding guard). Low Juliet complexity but low signal value for security evaluation purposes.

strategies.description: Combined pattern: introduce an integer overflow in a size computation that feeds into malloc, then overflow the resulting undersized buffer.
strategies.notes: Requires two-stage mutation (overflow the size, then overflow the buffer). Deferred to after single-mutation strategies are stable.
